title: Route Congestion Heat Windows
description: Find top three non-overlapping windows of size k with highest congestion score

examples.explanation: Step 1: Generate all windows of size 2. Windows: (score, start, end): (3,0,1), (5,1,2), (7,2,3), (9,3,4), (11,4,5), (13,5,6), (15,6,7), (17,7,8). Step 2: Try combinations of 3 non-overlapping windows. Check combination windows (17,7,8), (15,6,7), (13,5,6): Overlap between (15,6,7) and (17,7,8) at index 7, invalid. Check combination (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4): No overlap, total = 17+13+9 = 39. Check combination (15,6,7), (11,4,5), (7,2,3): No overlap, total = 15+11+7 = 33. Check combination (17,7,8), (11,4,5), (5,1,2): No overlap, total = 17+11+5 = 33. Check combination (13,5,6), (9,3,4), (5,1,2): No overlap, total = 13+9+5 = 27. Maximum found: 39 from windows (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4). Wait, let me check more combinations. Actually, the best combination is (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4) = 39. But let me verify all: Windows sorted by score: 17,15,13,11,9,7,5,3. Try (17,7,8), (15,6,7), (13,5,6): Overlap. (17,7,8), (15,6,7), (11,4,5): Overlap. (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (11,4,5): Overlap at 5-6 and 4-5? No, 5-6 and 4-5 are adjacent but not overlapping (4-5 ends at 5, 5-6 starts at 5, they share index 5? Actually window (4,5) covers indices 4,5. Window (5,6) covers indices 5,6. They share index 5, so they overlap. Invalid. (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4): No overlap, total 39. (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (7,2,3): No overlap, total 37. (17,7,8), (11,4,5), (9,3,4): Overlap at 4-5 and 3-4? Share index 4, invalid. (15,6,7), (13,5,6), (11,4,5): Overlap. (15,6,7), (13,5,6), (9,3,4): Overlap at 6-7 and 5-6? Share index 6, invalid. (15,6,7), (11,4,5), (9,3,4): Overlap at 4-5 and 3-4? Share index 4, invalid. (15,6,7), (11,4,5), (7,2,3): No overlap, total 33. (13,5,6), (11,4,5), (9,3,4): Overlap. (13,5,6), (11,4,5), (7,2,3): Overlap at 5-6 and 4-5? Share index 5, invalid. (13,5,6), (9,3,4), (7,2,3): No overlap, total 29. Maximum is 39.

Step 1: Generate all windows of size 1. Windows: (10,0,0), (20,1,1), (30,2,2), (40,3,3), (50,4,4). Step 2: Find top 3 non-overlapping windows. Since k=1, windows are single indices, non-overlapping means different indices. Simply pick the three highest values: 50, 40, 30. Total = 50+40+30 = 120. Check combinations: (50,4,4), (40,3,3), (30,2,2): No overlap, total 120. Any other combination gives lower total.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n k²)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible windows of size k, calculate congestion scores for each, then try all combinations of three non-overlapping windows to find the maximum total score. This approach is straightforward but extremely inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n k²)
ladders.desc: Find top three non-overlapping windows of size k with highest total congestion score using brute force O(nk²) approach.
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete problem solution using the brute force approach. The intuition is straightforward: generate all possible windows, calculate their congestion scores, then try every combination of three non-overlapping windows to find the maximum total score. While conceptually simple, this approach becomes computationally prohibitive for large inputs due to its O(nk²) time complexity. The algorithm works by first creating all possible windows of size k from the congestion
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Generate all windows of size 2. Windows: (score, start, end): (3,0,1), (5,1,2), (7,2,3), (9,3,4), (11,4,5), (13,5,6), (15,6,7), (17,7,8). Step 2: Try combinations of 3 non-overlapping windows. Check combination windows (17,7,8), (15,6,7), (13,5,6): Overlap between (15,6,7) and (17,7,8) at index 7, invalid. Chec
... (trace simplified)

Step 1: Generate all windows of size 1. Windows: (10,0,0), (20,1,1), (30,2,2), (40,3,3), (50,4,4). Step 2: Find top 3 non-overlapping windows. Since k=1, windows are single indices, non-overlapping means different indices. Simply pick the three highest values: 50, 40, 30. Total = 50+40+30 = 120. Check combinations: (50,4,4), (40,3,3), (30,2,2): No overlap, total 120. Any other combination gives lower total.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate and Score All Windows of Size k
ladders.desc: Generate all possible contiguous windows of size k from an array and calculate their congestion scores.
ladders.explanation:
This is the most important helper function for the brute force approach. The intuition is that to find the best windows, we first need to enumerate all possible windows and compute their scores. A window of size k is a contiguous subarray of length k. For an array of length n, there are n-k+1 such windows. The congestion score of a window is simply the sum of all elements within that window. This sub-routine is critical because it creates the pool of candidate windows from wh
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: n=5, k=3, number of windows = 5-3+1 = 3. Step 2: Window 1: start i=0, end j=2, indices 0,1,2, values 1,2,3, score = 1+2+3 = 6. Store (6,0,2). Step 3: Window 2: start i=1, end j=3, indices 1,2,3, values 2,3,4, score = 2+3+4 = 9. Store (9,1,3). Step 4: Window 3: start i=2, end j=4, indices 2,3,4, values 3,4,5, score = 3+4+5 = 12. Store (12,2,4). Step 5: Return list [(6,0,2), (9,1,3), (12,2,4)].

Step 1: n=3, k=2, number of windows = 3-2+1 = 2. Step 2: Window 1: start i=0, end j=1, indices 0,1, values 10,20, score = 10+20 = 30. Store (30,0,1). Step 3: Window 2: start i=1, end j=2, indices 1,2, values 20,30, score = 20+30 = 50. Store (50,1,2). Step 4: Return list [(30,0,1), (50,1,2)].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sliding Window Sum Calculation
ladders.desc: Calculate the sum of elements in a sliding window of fixed size k as it moves through an array.
ladders.explanation:
This is the central technique for efficiently calculating window scores in the brute force approach. The intuition is that instead of recalculating each window sum from scratch (which would be O(k) per window), we can use a sliding window approach to compute consecutive window sums in O(1) time each after the first window. However, in the brute force O(nk²) approach, we actually use the naive O(nk) method for simplicity, but understanding the sliding window optimization is cr
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Calculate first window (indices 0,1,2): sum = 1+2+3 = 6. Store 6. Step 2: Slide window to start at i=1: left_index = 0 (value 1 leaves), right_index = 3 (value 4 enters). New sum = 6 - 1 + 4 = 9. Store 9. Step 3: Slide window to start at i=2: left_index = 1 (value 2 leaves), right_index = 4 (value 5 enters). New sum = 9 - 2 + 5 = 12. Store 12. Step 4: Result list [6,9,12].

Step 1: First window (indices 0,1): sum = 10+20 = 30. Store 30. Step 2: Slide to start at i=1: left_index = 0 (10 leaves), right_index = 2 (30 enters). New sum = 30 - 10 + 30 = 50. Store 50. Step 3: Slide to start at i=2: left_index = 1 (20 leaves), right_index = 3 (40 enters). New sum = 50 - 20 + 40 = 70. Store 70. Step 4: Result [30,50,70].

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal and Subarray Extraction
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array and extract contiguous subarrays of a specified length.
ladders.explanation:
This intermediate concept is needed before implementing window operations. The intuition is that to work with windows (contiguous subarrays), we need to be able to traverse the array and extract portions of it. This involves understanding array indexing, loop boundaries, and subarray slicing. A subarray is defined by its start and end indices, and it includes all elements between them. For a window of size k starting at index i, the subarray consists of elements at indices i,
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: n=4, k=2, last start index = 4-2 = 2. Step 2: i=0: Create subarray from indices 0 to 1: elements arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2 → [1,2]. Step 3: i=1: Create subarray from indices 1 to 2: elements arr[1]=2, arr[2]=3 → [2,3]. Step 4: i=2: Create subarray from indices 2 to 3: elements arr[2]=3, arr[3]=4 → [3,4]. Step 5: Return [[1,2], [2,3], [3,4]].
Step 1: n=3, k=3, last start index = 3-3 = 0. Step 2: i=0: Create subarray from indices 0 to 2: elements arr[0]=10, arr[1]=20, arr[2]=30 → [10,20,30]. Step 3: Return [[10,20,30]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access and Summation
ladders.desc: Access individual elements in an array and compute their sum.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation is used throughout the window congestion problem. The intuition is simple: to calculate a window's congestion score, we need to access each element in the window and add them together. Arrays are zero-indexed data structures where elements are stored at consecutive indices. Accessing an element at index i is typically an O(1) operation. Summation involves initializing a total to 0, then iterating through the elements, adding each one to the total. T
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize total = 0. Step 2: i=1: total = 0 + arr[1] = 0 + 2 = 2. Step 3: i=2: total = 2 + arr[2] = 2 + 3 = 5. Step 4: i=3: total = 5 + arr[3] = 5 + 4 = 9. Step 5: Return 9.
Step 1: Initialize total = 0. Step 2: i=0: total = 0 + arr[0] = 0 + 10 = 10. Step 3: i=1: total = 10 + arr[1] = 10 + 20 = 30. Step 4: i=2: total = 30 + arr[2] = 30 + 30 = 60. Step 5: Return 60.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Arrays store multiple values accessible by index starting from 0.
ladders.explanation: An array is an ordered collection where each element is stored at a specific index (starting from 0). This allows systematic access and modification of elements — essential for finding top three non-overlapping windows with highest congestion scores.

examples.trace: Array has 5 elements. Indices: 0,1,2,3,4. arr[2] = 3.

approaches.name: Heap-based Three Pass Selection O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Precompute window scores using sliding window, then use a max-heap to select top windows in three passes while marking selected windows and their neighbors as unavailable. This greedy-like approach efficiently finds high-scoring non-overlapping windows.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Heap-based Three Pass Selection O(n log n)
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find top three non-overlapping windows of size k with highest congestion scores using sliding window and max-heap.
ladders.explanation:
This solution addresses the core challenge of selecting high-scoring windows without overlaps. The intuition is that we need to greedily pick the best available windows while respecting non-overlap constraints. First, we precompute congestion scores for all windows of size k using a sliding window technique, which is efficient. Then, we use a max-heap to select windows in three passes: each pass extracts the highest-scoring window from the heap, adds it to the result, and mar
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Precompute window scores for size k=2: windows are indices 0-1: score 3+1=4, 1-2: 1+4=5, 2-3: 4+2=6, 3-4: 2+5=7. Scores: [(4,0), (5,1), (6,2), (7,3)] where tuple is (score, start_index). Step 2: Build max-heap: [(7,3), (6,2), (5,1), (4,0)]. Step 3: First pass: pop (7,3), add to result, mark index 3 and neighbor
... (trace simplified)

Step 1: Precompute scores for k=3: windows: index 0-2: 10+20+30=60, 1-3: 20+30+40=90, 2-4: 30+40+50=120. Scores: [(60,0), (90,1), (120,2)]. Step 2: Max-heap: [(120,2), (90,1), (60,0)]. Step 3: First pass: pop (120,2), add to result, mark index 2 and neighbors (0,1,3,4) as unavailable? Overlap if start indices differ by
... (trace simplified)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Heap-based Selection with Neighbor Marking
ladders.desc: Select top m non-overlapping items from a list using a max-heap and marking neighbors as unavailable.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the full problem, focusing on how to greedily pick high-value items without overlaps. The intuition is that by using a max-heap, we can efficiently access the highest-scoring item, and by marking its neighbors as unavailable, we enforce non-overlap constraints. This mimics a greedy algorithm that works well for interval scheduling problems. Steps: 1. Build a max-heap from items with their scores and indices. 2. Initialize an empty result list 
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Build max-heap: [(40,3), (30,2), (20,1), (10,0)]. Step 2: Result=[], unavailable={}. Step 3: First pass: pop (40,3), index 3 not unavailable, add to result: [(40,3)]. Mark index 3 and neighbors within overlap_dist=1: indices 2,3,4. Unavailable={2,3,4}. Step 4: Second pass: pop (30,2), index 2 is unavailable, sk
... (trace simplified)

Step 1: Max-heap: [(5,2), (5,1), (5,0)] (ties broken by index, but heap order may vary; assume sorted). Step 2: Result=[], unavailable={}. Step 3: First pass: pop (5,2), add to result: [(5,2)]. Mark index 2 and neighbors within 2: indices 0,1,2,3,4. Unavailable={0,1,2,3,4}. Step 4: Second pass: heap has [(5,1), (5,0)],

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Selection with Constraint Enforcement via Marking
ladders.desc: The technique of iteratively picking the best available option while invalidating conflicting options.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic underpins many optimization problems where we need to select items under constraints. The intuition is that by always choosing the highest-value item and then eliminating overlapping choices, we can efficiently approximate or achieve optimal solutions, especially in interval scheduling or resource allocation. This greedy approach works when the constraint is that selected items cannot overlap, and it often yields good results if not always optimal, but in this
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.input: items = [('A',10), ('B',8), ('C',6), ('D',4)], conflicts: A conflicts with B, C; B conflicts with A, C; C conflicts with A, B, D; D conflicts with C. Select top 2.
examples.trace: Step 1: Sort by value: [('A',10), ('B',8), ('C',6), ('D',4)]. Step 2: Select A (value 10), mark A and its conflicts B,C as unavailable. Available: D. Step 3: Select next highest available: D (value 4), mark D and its conflict C as unavailable (C already unavailable). Step 4: Have 2 items, stop. Output: [('A',10), ('D',4)].

examples.input: items = [('X',5), ('Y',5), ('Z',5)], conflicts: X conflicts with Y; Y conflicts with X, Z; Z conflicts with Y. Select top 3.
examples.trace: Step 1: Items all value 5, assume order X,Y,Z. Step 2: Select X, mark X and conflict Y as unavailable. Available: Z. Step 3: Select Z, mark Z and conflict Y as unavailable (Y already unavailable). Step 4: Need top 3, but only 2 available due to conflicts. Output: [('X',5), ('Z',5)].

testCases.input: items = [('P',100), ('Q',90), ('R',80)], conflicts: P conflicts with Q; Q conflicts with P,R; R conflicts with Q. Select top 2.
items = [('M',7), ('N',6), ('O',5)], conflicts: M conflicts with N; N conflicts with M,O; O conflicts with N. Select top 1.
items = [('S',3), ('T',3), ('U',3)], conflicts: S conflicts with T; T conflicts with S,U; U conflicts with T. Select top 3.
items = [('V',10), ('W',9)], conflicts: V conflicts with W; W conflicts with V. Select top 2.
items = [('A',5), ('B',4), ('C',3)], conflicts: A conflicts with B,C; B conflicts with A; C conflicts with A. Select top 2.
items = [('F',6), ('G',5), ('H',4), ('I',3)], conflicts: F conflicts with G; G conflicts with F,H; H conflicts with G,I; I conflicts with H. Select top 3.
items = [('J',8), ('K',7), ('L',6)], conflicts: J conflicts with K,L; K conflicts with J; L conflicts with J. Select top 2.
items = [('X',9), ('Y',8), ('Z',7)], conflicts: X conflicts with Y; Y conflicts with X; Z conflicts with none. Select top 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sliding Window Sum Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of all contiguous subarrays of fixed size k in an array.
ladders.explanation:
This building block is essential for precomputing congestion scores for each window. The intuition is that instead of recomputing sums from scratch for each window, we can slide a window across the array and update the sum efficiently by adding the new element and subtracting the old one. This reduces time complexity from O(n*k) to O(n). Steps: 1. Calculate the sum of the first k elements. 2. Store this as the score for the first window (starting at index 0). 3. For each subs
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Initial sum for first window (indices 0-2): 1+2+3 = 6. Step 2: Slide to window starting at index 1: subtract arr[0]=1, add arr[3]=4, new sum = 6 - 1 + 4 = 9. Step 3: Slide to window starting at index 2: subtract arr[1]=2, add arr[4]=5, new sum = 9 - 2 + 5 = 12. Step 4: No more windows (n=5, k=3, last start index is 2). Output: [6, 9, 12].
Step 1: Initial sum for window 0-1: 10+20 = 30. Step 2: Slide to window starting at index 1: subtract arr[0]=10, add arr[2]=30, new sum = 30 - 10 + 30 = 50. Step 3: Output: [30, 50].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Max-Heap Insertion and Extraction
ladders.desc: Insert elements into a max-heap and extract the maximum element efficiently.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation is fundamental for maintaining a collection where we need frequent access to the largest element. A max-heap is a complete binary tree where each node is greater than or equal to its children, allowing O(log n) insertion and extraction. Intuition: By organizing data in a heap, we can quickly retrieve the max value without sorting the entire list each time. Steps: For insertion: 1. Add the new element at the end of the heap. 2. Bubble it up by comparing wi
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.input: Insert into empty max-heap: 5, 3, 8, 1, then extract max.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Insert 5: heap [5]. Step 2: Insert 3: add at end [5,3], bubble up: 3 <= 5, no swap. Heap [5,3]. Step 3: Insert 8: add at end [5,3,8], bubble up: compare 8 with parent 5, swap -> [8,3,5]. Step 4: Insert 1: add at end [8,3,5,1], bubble up: 1 <= parent 5, no swap. Heap [8,3,5,1]. Step 5: Extract max: remove root 8
... (trace simplified)

Step 1: Initial heap [10,7,9,5] (assumed max-heap property). Step 2: Extract max: remove 10, move last 5 to root [5,7,9], bubble down: compare 5 with children 7 and 9, swap with larger 9 -> [9,7,5]. Output first extract: 10. Step 3: Extract max from [9,7,5]: remove 9, move last 5 to root [5,7], bubble down: compare 5 with child 7, swap -> [7,5]. Output second extract: 9.

testCases.input: Insert into empty: 4, 2, 6, then extract max.
Insert into empty: 1, 2, 3, 4, extract max twice.
Insert into empty: 7, 3, 9, 1, extract max, then insert 8.
Heap [12, 11, 10], extract max, insert 13, extract max.
